title: Standard Precautions and Transmission-Based Precautions
learningObjectives: Discuss the three most common bloodborne viral illnesses to which providers may be exposed.
Discuss signs/symptoms of airborne and droplet-transmitted diseases and protective measures.
Describe precautions to prevent exposure to blood and other potentially infectious materials.
Describe the steps to take after an exposure, including post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP).

summary.title: Standard Precautions
summary.content: Standard precautions treat the blood and body fluids of every patient as potentially infectious. They include hand hygiene, gloves, and—when splashing is possible—face/eye protection, gowns, and masks. Apply them universally regardless of a patient's known diagnosis, because infection status is often unknown. They protect against exposure to blood and other potentially infectious materials (OPIM).

summary.title: Bloodborne Viral Illnesses
summary.content: The three most important bloodborne viral illnesses for providers are hepatitis B virus (HBV), hepatitis C virus (HCV), and human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). HBV is the most transmissible of the three and is vaccine-preventable—providers should be vaccinated. HCV has no vaccine. HIV has a lower transmission risk per exposure but serious consequences. All are spread through blood and certain body fluids via needlesticks, mucous membranes, or non-intact skin.

summary.title: Transmission-Based Precautions
summary.content: When standard precautions are not enough, add transmission-based precautions: Contact precautions (gowns/gloves for organisms spread by touch), Droplet precautions (a surgical mask for diseases spread by large respiratory droplets, e.g., influenza, meningococcus), and Airborne precautions (a fit-tested N95/respirator and patient masking for small-particle diseases such as tuberculosis, measles, and varicella). Recognize symptoms (cough, fever, rash) and apply the right level early.

summary.title: Tuberculosis and the BCG Note
summary.content: Tuberculosis (TB) is spread by the airborne route; suspect it in patients with chronic cough, fever, night sweats, and weight loss, and use airborne precautions (N95 for the provider, surgical mask on the patient). The bacille Calmette-Guérin (BCG) vaccine, used in some countries, can cause a positive TB skin test, complicating screening—interpretation requires clinical context.

summary.title: Post-Exposure Management
summary.content: After a significant exposure (needlestick, splash to mucous membranes or non-intact skin): immediately wash the site with soap and water (flush mucous membranes with water/saline), report the exposure per protocol, and seek evaluation promptly. Post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) for HIV is time-sensitive and most effective when started as soon as possible (within hours). HBV-vaccinated providers have strong protection; HBV immune globulin/vaccination and follow-up testing are arranged as indicated. There is no PEP for HCV, but early identification allows monitoring and treatment.

clinicalPearls: Treat every patient's blood and body fluids as potentially infectious—apply standard precautions universally.
HBV is vaccine-preventable—be vaccinated; HCV and HIV have no vaccine.
Match the precaution to the route: Contact (touch), Droplet (surgical mask), Airborne (fit-tested N95).
Suspect TB with chronic cough/fever/night sweats/weight loss—use airborne precautions early.
Start HIV post-exposure prophylaxis as soon as possible after a significant exposure—time matters.
redFlags: A needlestick or mucous-membrane splash is a true exposure—wash, report, and seek evaluation immediately.
Delaying HIV PEP reduces its effectiveness—it is time-sensitive.
Failing to don a fit-tested N95 for a suspected TB/measles patient risks airborne transmission.
Assuming a patient is 'low risk' and skipping precautions—infection status is frequently unknown.
commonMistakes: Selectively applying precautions based on assumptions about a patient instead of universally.
Using a surgical mask when a fit-tested N95 (airborne) is required.
Delaying reporting and PEP after an exposure.
Not being vaccinated against hepatitis B.
memoryAids: Three bloodborne viruses: HBV (vaccine), HCV (no vaccine), HIV (time-sensitive PEP).
Transmission precautions: Contact (gown/gloves), Droplet (surgical mask), Airborne (N95).
Airborne diseases: TB, Measles, Varicella ('My Chicken-pox TB').
After exposure: Wash → Report → Evaluate → PEP (start HIV PEP ASAP).

decisionTrees.title: Precautions and Exposure Response
decisionTrees.content:
1. Every patient → standard precautions (hand hygiene, gloves; add eye/face/gown for splash risk).
2. Suspected transmissible disease → add precautions: Contact (touch organisms), Droplet (surgical mask: influenza, meningococcus), Airborne (N95 + patient mask: TB, measles, varicella).
3. Exposure occurs (needlestick/splash) → wash site with soap and water / flush mucous membranes.
4. Report per protocol and seek evaluation immediately.
5. Start HIV PEP as soon as possible; arrange HBV prophylaxis/testing as indicated; monitor for HCV (no PEP).

references: International Trauma Life Support, 8th ed. Chapter 22: Standard Precautions and Transmission-Based Precautions.
CDC. Guideline for Isolation Precautions and Bloodborne Pathogen Standards (OSHA).
